meta.name: PDSS-SR Panic Disorder Severity Scale
meta.description: The PDSS-SR is a 7-item self-report scale that measures the overall severity of panic disorder, including panic attack frequency, distress, anticipatory anxiety, avoidance behaviors, and functional impairment.
meta.author: Houck, Spiegel, Shear & Rucci (2002)
meta.reference: Houck PR, Spiegel DA, Shear MK, Rucci P. Reliability of the self-report version of the Panic Disorder Severity Scale. Depress Anxiety. 2002;15(4):183-185.
meta.instruction: Please rate each of the following items based on your experience over the past week. Select the option that best describes your situation.

questions.text: How many panic attacks did you have during the past week?
If you had any panic attacks during the past week, how distressing were they?
During the past week, how much have you worried or felt anxious about when your next panic attack would occur?
During the past week, were there any places or situations you avoided or felt afraid of because of fear of a panic attack?
During the past week, were there any activities, sensations, or experiences that you avoided because they reminded you of a panic attack?
During the past week, how much did panic and related symptoms interfere with your ability to work or carry out your responsibilities?
During the past week, how much did panic and related symptoms interfere with your social life?

ranges.label: Minimal Panic Symptoms
ranges.description: Your score suggests minimal or no significant panic disorder symptoms over the past week. Panic attacks may be absent or very infrequent with little associated distress or avoidance. Continue maintaining healthy coping strategies that work for you.
suggestions: Continue maintaining healthy coping strategies and stress management practices
Practice regular relaxation techniques such as deep breathing or progressive muscle relaxation as preventive measures
If occasional anxiety arises, remind yourself that panic attacks, while distressing, are not medically dangerous

ranges.label: Mild to Moderate Panic
ranges.description: Your score indicates mild to moderate panic disorder symptoms, including some panic attacks, anticipatory anxiety, and avoidance. While not severely disabling, these symptoms may be causing meaningful distress and limiting certain activities.
suggestions: Learn about the cognitive model of panic: panic attacks are caused by catastrophic misinterpretation of bodily sensations, not actual danger
Practice diaphragmatic breathing and interoceptive exposure exercises to reduce fear of physical sensations
Consider cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), which is the gold-standard treatment for panic disorder
Keep a panic diary to identify triggers, early warning signs, and patterns in your panic attacks

ranges.label: Moderate to Severe Panic
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate to severe panic disorder symptoms that are likely impacting your daily life, work, and social activities. Avoidance behaviors may be restricting your activities and anticipatory anxiety may be causing persistent worry.
suggestions: Professional treatment is strongly recommended; CBT with exposure therapy is highly effective for panic disorder
Discuss medication options with a psychiatrist, as SSRIs and SNRIs can significantly reduce panic symptoms
Gradually challenge avoidance behaviors with professional guidance, as avoidance maintains the panic cycle
Build a support network of trusted people who understand your condition and can provide encouragement
Avoid relying on safety behaviors or substances such as alcohol or benzodiazepines to cope with panic

ranges.label: Severe Panic
ranges.description: Your score indicates severe panic disorder symptoms with significant functional impairment across multiple life domains. You may experience frequent panic attacks, intense anticipatory anxiety, and extensive avoidance. Prompt treatment is essential.
suggestions: Please seek professional help as soon as possible; severe panic disorder is highly treatable with proper intervention
If you feel you are in crisis or unable to cope, contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline for immediate support
A combination of CBT and medication (such as SSRIs) is often the most effective approach for severe panic disorder
Do not avoid situations out of fear; avoidance reinforces the panic cycle and leads to increasing restriction of activities
Remember that panic disorder has among the highest treatment success rates of any mental health condition
